topic: Electrochemistry — Electrolysis, Electroplating, Galvanic Cells & Batteries

questions.text: During electrolysis, oxidation takes place at the:
questions.explanation: Oxidation (loss of electrons) always occurs at the anode; reduction (gain of electrons) at the cathode. Remember: 'An Ox Red Cat' — Anode = Oxidation; Reduction = Cathode. This holds true for both electrolytic AND galvanic cells.

questions.text: The electrolyte used in a lead-acid car battery is:
questions.explanation: Lead-acid batteries (used in cars and inverters) use dilute sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) as the electrolyte. The anode is lead (Pb) and cathode is lead dioxide (PbO₂). Each cell produces 2 V; a 12-V car battery has 6 cells.

questions.text: In electroplating, the article to be plated is made the:
questions.explanation: The article to be plated is always the cathode (connected to the negative terminal). Reduction occurs at the cathode, depositing metal ions from the electrolyte solution onto the object's surface. The anode is the metal used for coating.

questions.text: Which of the following is a primary (non-rechargeable) battery?
questions.explanation: The dry cell (Leclanché cell) is a primary battery — used once and discarded. Its anode is a zinc case, cathode is a carbon rod surrounded by MnO₂, and the electrolyte is NH₄Cl paste. Lead-acid, Li-ion, and Ni-Cd are all secondary (rechargeable) batteries.

questions.text: In a Daniell cell, which electrode dissolves during the cell reaction?
questions.explanation: In the Daniell cell (Zn-Cu), the zinc anode dissolves: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻. At the copper cathode, Cu²⁺ ions from the CuSO₄ solution are reduced and deposited: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. So the zinc rod shrinks and the copper rod grows during operation.

questions.text: If iron is coated with tin (tinning) and the coating is scratched, which metal corrodes first?
options: Iron corrodes because tin is less reactive than iron
Both corrode at the same rate
Neither corrodes — scratching has no effect
questions.explanation: Tin is LESS reactive than iron (lower in reactivity series). If tin coating is scratched, iron becomes the anode (more reactive) and corrodes. This is the opposite of galvanisation (zinc is MORE reactive than iron, so zinc acts as sacrificial anode even when scratched). Tin cans are fine as long as the coating is intact.

questions.text: The 'salt bridge' in a galvanic cell serves to:
options: Increase the voltage of the cell
Prevent the electrodes from reacting with each other
Maintain electrical neutrality by allowing ion migration between half-cells
Supply additional electrolyte to the cell
questions.explanation: As current flows, one half-cell accumulates positive ions (anode compartment) and the other accumulates negative ions (cathode compartment). Without a salt bridge, this charge build-up would stop the reaction. The salt bridge (KCl or KNO₃ in agar gel) allows ions to migrate and maintain electrical neutrality.

questions.text: During the charging of a lead-acid battery, the cathode is:
questions.explanation: During charging, an external power supply reverses the spontaneous discharge reaction. During charging, PbSO₄ at the 'cathode' is reduced back to PbO₂ (it becomes the cathode of the charging process). During discharge, PbO₂ is the positive electrode (cathode). The discharged state has both plates as PbSO₄.

questions.text: The Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2019 was awarded for the development of which type of battery?
questions.explanation: The 2019 Nobel Prize in Chemistry went to John B. Goodenough, M. Stanley Whittingham, and Akira Yoshino for the development of lithium-ion batteries. Li-ion batteries power smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles — a foundational technology of the modern world.

questions.text: The by-product of a hydrogen fuel cell is:
questions.explanation: A hydrogen fuel cell combines hydrogen (fuel) and oxygen to produce electricity. The only by-product is water (H₂O). This makes fuel cells a clean energy technology. They are used in NASA space missions and hydrogen-powered vehicles (e.g., Toyota Mirai).

questions.text: Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis states that the mass of substance deposited is proportional to:
options: The quantity of electricity passed (charge = current × time)
questions.explanation: Faraday's First Law: m ∝ Q, where Q = I × t (charge = current in amperes × time in seconds). To deposit twice the mass, either double the current or double the time. Michael Faraday (1791–1867) also discovered electromagnetic induction.

questions.text: In the electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution), what product is formed at the cathode?
questions.explanation: In the Chlor-Alkali process (electrolysis of brine): Cathode: 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂↑ + 2OH⁻ (hydrogen gas). Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂↑ + 2e⁻ (chlorine gas). NaOH also forms in solution. These three — NaOH, Cl₂, H₂ — are all commercially valuable.

questions.text: Which metal is used for galvanisation to protect iron from rusting?
questions.explanation: Galvanisation = coating iron/steel with zinc. Zinc is more reactive than iron (higher in reactivity series), so it acts as a sacrificial anode — even if the zinc coating is scratched, zinc corrodes preferentially, protecting the iron underneath. Tinning uses tin, which is less reactive (iron corrodes if scratched).

questions.text: The voltage produced by a single cell of a lead-acid battery is:
questions.explanation: Each cell of a lead-acid battery produces 2 V. A standard 12-V car battery contains 6 cells connected in series: 6 × 2 V = 12 V. Dry cells produce 1.5 V per cell; lithium-ion cells produce about 3.6 V per cell.

questions.text: The rusting of iron requires the simultaneous presence of:
questions.explanation: Rusting of iron is an electrochemical process requiring BOTH oxygen (O₂) AND moisture (water, H₂O). Iron in dry air does NOT rust. Iron in de-aerated (oxygen-free) water does NOT rust. Salt (electrolyte) speeds up rusting but is not essential.

questions.text: Alessandro Volta invented the first electric battery (Voltaic Pile) in:
questions.explanation: Alessandro Volta constructed the Voltaic Pile in 1800 — stacked zinc and silver discs separated by brine-soaked cloth. It was the world's first electric battery. 1836: Daniell cell; 1859: Planté's lead-acid battery. The unit of electric potential (volt) is named after Volta.
